skill.name: terraform-skill
skill.description: Terraform infrastructure as code best practices
skill.summary: Comprehensive Terraform and OpenTofu guidance for testing, modules, CI/CD, and production patterns
skill.author: Anton Babenko

security_audit.summary: All static findings are false positives. This is a legitimate Terraform best practices skill providing infrastructure-as-code guidance. The 85 'external_commands' detections are Ruby template syntax in markdown rendering HCL code examples. Network detections are legitimate reference URLs and example CIDR blocks in documentation. Sensitive data and cryptographic algorithm warnings refer to security best practices being taught, not actual vulnerabilities.

low_findings.title: Documentation Contains Example IP Addresses
low_findings.description: The skill contains example CIDR blocks (10.0.0.0/16, 10.1.0.0/24) in Terraform code examples. These are standard documentation practice for showing network configurations and pose no security risk.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: These are clearly labeled example CIDR blocks in code examples, standard documentation practice, not actual production IP addresses

content.value_statement: This skill helps developers write production-ready Terraform code with proper structure, testing, and security. It provides guidance on module design, CI/CD integration, and infrastructure best practices based on enterprise experience.
actual_capabilities: Guide users on Terraform project structure and module hierarchy
Recommend appropriate testing strategies (validate, plan, Terratest, native tests)
Provide best practices for variable naming, resource ordering, and code organization
Explain count vs for_each selection and when to use each
Guide on CI/CD integration with GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, and Atlantis
Cover security scanning with Trivy and Checkov
limitations: Does not provide cloud-specific API documentation (links to official docs instead)
Does not execute Terraform commands (user must run them separately)
Does not have access to user Terraform state or credentials
Does not replace understanding of Terraform syntax basics

use_cases.title: New Infrastructure Project Setup
use_cases.description: A DevOps engineer starting a new Terraform project needs guidance on directory structure, module organization, and naming conventions to ensure maintainable code from the start.

use_cases.title: Testing Strategy Selection
use_cases.description: A team wants to add automated testing to their Terraform workflow but needs help choosing between native tests, Terratest, and static analysis tools based on their use case and expertise.

use_cases.title: Module Refactoring
use_cases.description: An existing Terraform configuration needs refactoring to use modern patterns like for_each, moved blocks, or better variable design to improve maintainability.

prompt_templates.title: Initial Project Structure
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me set up a new Terraform project with proper structure for a multi-environment deployment. I need to understand the recommended directory layout for environments, modules, and examples.
prompt_templates.scenario: Starting a new Terraform project and need guidance on best practices

prompt_templates.title: Testing Framework Selection
prompt_templates.prompt: What testing approach should I use for my Terraform modules? I have simple validation logic and some complex resource configurations. Looking for cost-effective testing that balances coverage with maintenance effort.
prompt_templates.scenario: Choosing the right testing strategy for Terraform code

prompt_templates.title: Module Design Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my module structure and suggest improvements. I have a VPC module that creates subnets, security groups, and routing. Should I split this into smaller modules or keep it as one?

prompt_templates.title: CI/CD Pipeline Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a CI/CD pipeline for Terraform that includes validation, security scanning, planning, and apply stages. I want to optimize for cost and ensure proper approvals for production changes.
prompt_templates.scenario: Setting up automated workflows for infrastructure changes

output_examples.input: Help me understand when to use count vs for_each in Terraform
output_examples.output: Use count when you have a boolean condition (create or not) or a fixed number of identical resources. Use for_each when items may be reordered, removed, or you need stable addressing by key. For example, use for_each = toset(var.availability_zones) for subnets so removing an AZ doesn't recreate all subsequent subnets.

output_examples.input: What is the recommended variable ordering in Terraform?
output_examples.output: Always order variable blocks consistently: description (required), type, default, validation, nullable. This ensures clarity and helps users understand the variable purpose immediately. Always include a description for every variable.

output_examples.input: How should I structure my Terraform module?
output_examples.output: A well-structured module includes: main.tf (resources), variables.tf (inputs), outputs.tf (outputs), versions.tf (provider constraints), examples/ (usage examples), and tests/ (test files). Keep modules small and focused on a single responsibility.

best_practices: Use descriptive, contextual names for resources (e.g., web_server, application_logs) and 'this' for singleton resources
Always include descriptions for variables and outputs to aid future maintainers
Pin provider versions using pessimistic constraints (e.g., ~> 5.0) to balance stability with updates
anti_patterns: Avoid generic resource names like 'main', 'bucket', or 'instance' for non-singletons
Do not store secrets in Terraform variables; use secret managers instead
Avoid default VPCs and overly permissive security groups (0.0.0.0/0)

faq.question: Does this skill work with OpenTofu as well?
faq.answer: Yes, this skill supports both Terraform and OpenTofu. All guidance applies to both tools unless specifically noted.

faq.question: Can this skill execute Terraform commands for me?
faq.answer: No, this skill provides guidance and best practices. You need to run Terraform commands (init, plan, apply) in your own environment.

faq.question: What testing frameworks does this skill cover?
faq.answer: It covers terraform validate, terraform test (native 1.6+), Terratest, and security tools like Trivy and Checkov.

faq.question: Does this skill help with security scanning?
faq.answer: Yes, it provides guidance on integrating Trivy and Checkov for infrastructure security scanning in CI/CD pipelines.

faq.question: What Terraform versions are supported?
faq.answer: The skill covers Terraform 1.0+ through 1.11+, highlighting features available in each version like native testing (1.6+), mock providers (1.7+), and write-only arguments (1.11+).

faq.question: How does this skill handle cost optimization?
faq.answer: It recommends using mocking for PR validation, running integration tests only on main, implementing auto-cleanup, and tagging test resources to track spending.
